questions.question: Name three production scenarios where an LLM routing layer earns its place.
options: Code formatting, linting, testing
Per-request cost routing (Haiku for triage, Sonnet for synthesis), automatic failover when a provider has a bad hour, and region-pinned routing for compliance
Caching, minification, bundling
Fine-tuning, distillation, quantization
questions.explanation: Cost, failover, latency SLAs, compliance regions, and A/B experimentation are the five scenarios — all repetitive to hand-code per integration, all solved once at the gateway.

questions.question: Why do routing gateways expose an OpenAI-compatible API?
options: OpenAI requires it contractually
Everyone already speaks the OpenAI schema, so the gateway accepts it at /v1/chat/completions and proxies internally to Anthropic, Gemini, or anything else — clients don't change
It is the only schema that supports streaming
Other formats are deprecated
questions.explanation: The OpenAI shape is the de-facto lingua franca. The gateway translates at the edge, which is also what makes model aliases work: your code says our_smart_model and the mapping changes server-side.

questions.question: What guard keeps a fallback chain (gpt-4o → claude → gemini) from exploding costs?
options: Fallbacks are free under provider agreements
Retries count against a budget, so cascading failovers stop instead of multiplying spend
Only one fallback is ever allowed
The chain only runs at night
questions.explanation: Failover on 5xx is automatic, but each retry consumes a defined budget. Without that, a provider outage turns into N-times cost amplification across the chain.

questions.question: How does semantic caching differ from exact-match caching, and what does it save?
options: It caches by URL; saves bandwidth only
Keys are embedding-based so near-identical prompts share a slot — repeated agent loops save 30-60%
It stores responses in the model's weights
It only works for embeddings endpoints
questions.explanation: Agent loops repeat nearly-identical prompts constantly. Embedding-keyed caching catches paraphrases that exact-match misses, which is where the 30-60% savings come from.

questions.question: Which routing option fits a team that needs guardrails and compliance out of the box?
options: LiteLLM — it ships mandatory guardrails
Portkey — production-grade with PII redaction and full OTel; LiteLLM suits self-hosted control, OpenRouter suits rapid prototyping
OpenRouter — compliance is its core feature
None — guardrails must always be custom
questions.explanation: The 2026 archetypes: LiteLLM (open-source, your keys, data sovereignty, needs an SRE team), OpenRouter (managed SaaS, 300+ models, fastest start), Portkey (open-sourced March 2026, opinionated guardrails + compliance).
